section.title: The House of Benjamin and Saul
section.sectionRationale: This chapter completes the Chronicler's long genealogical introduction by returning to the tribe of Benjamin in detail, tracing its families all the way down to Saul's own household. For the Chronicler's first audience, the returned exiles, Benjamin was no obscure footnote: it was, with Judah, one of the two tribes that actually resettled the land and rebuilt Jerusalem, so this list roots that audience in a family line that runs both to a king who failed and, through their own continuing place among God's people, into the ongoing story of hope.
section.thesis: Benjamin's family tree, patiently traced down to Saul's own household, quietly prepares the reader for the tragedy of chapters 9 and 10: this is the line of the king who failed, yet Benjamin's continuing presence among the resettled remnant shows that God does not discard a tribe for its king's sin, but keeps a place for it in the ongoing story that runs to David and, at last, to Christ.
section.frameNote: Seven family groups: the sons of Benjamin; Ehud's descendants and the exile to Manahath; Shaharaim's Moabite-born sons; the sons of Elpaal; the families of Shimei and Shashak; the heads of families who settled in Jerusalem and Gibeon; and, climactically, the genealogy of Saul himself, down to the skilled archers of Ulam's line.

verses.text: Benjamin was the father of Bela his firstborn, Ashbel the second, Aharah the third,
Nohah the fourth, and Rapha the fifth.
The sons of Bela: Addar, Gera, Abihud,
Abishua, Naaman, Ahoah,
Gera, Shephuphan, and Huram.

units.label: Ehud's descendants and the exile to Manahath
units.note: a family already scattered before Israel's exile

verses.text: These were the descendants of Ehud who were the heads of the families living in Geba and were exiled to Manahath:
Naaman, Ahijah, and Gera, who carried them into exile and who was the father of Uzza and Ahihud.
Shaharaim had sons in the country of Moab after he had divorced his wives Hushim and Baara.
His sons by his wife Hodesh: Jobab, Zibia, Mesha, Malcam,
Jeuz, Sachia, and Mirmah. These were his sons, heads of families.
He also had sons by Hushim: Abitub and Elpaal.
The sons of Elpaal: Eber, Misham, Shemed (who built Ono and Lod with its villages),
and Beriah and Shema (who were the heads of families living in Aijalon and who drove out the inhabitants of Gath).
Ahio, Shashak, Jeremoth,
Zebadiah, Arad, Eder,
Michael, Ishpah, and Joha were the sons of Beriah.
Zebadiah, Meshullam, Hizki, Heber,
Ishmerai, Izliah, and Jobab were the sons of Elpaal.

units.label: The families of Shimei and Shashak

verses.text: Jakim, Zichri, Zabdi,
Elienai, Zillethai, Eliel,
Adaiah, Beraiah, and Shimrath were the sons of Shimei.
Ishpan, Eber, Eliel,
Abdon, Zichri, Hanan,
Hananiah, Elam, Anthothijah,
Iphdeiah, and Penuel were the sons of Shashak.
Shamsherai, Shehariah, Athaliah,
Jaareshiah, Elijah, and Zichri were the sons of Jeroham.

units.label: Heads of families in Jerusalem and Gibeon

verses.text: All these were heads of families, the chiefs according to their {{yachas|genealogies}}, and they lived in Jerusalem.
Jeiel the father of Gibeon lived in Gibeon. His wife's name was Maacah,
and Abdon was his firstborn son, then Zur, Kish, Baal, Nadab,
Gedor, Ahio, Zecher,
and Mikloth, who was the father of Shimeah. They too lived alongside their relatives in Jerusalem.

units.label: Saul's genealogy
units.note: the family of the king who failed

verses.text: Ner was the father of Kish, Kish was the father of Saul, and Saul was the father of Jonathan, Malchishua, Abinadab, and {{baal|Esh-baal}}.
The son of Jonathan: {{baal|Merib-baal}}, and Merib-baal was the father of Micah.
The sons of Micah: Pithon, Melech, Tarea, and Ahaz.
Ahaz was the father of Jehoaddah, Jehoaddah was the father of Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri, and Zimri was the father of Moza.
Moza was the father of Binea. Raphah was his son, Eleasah his son, and Azel his son.
Azel had six sons, and these were their names: Azrikam, Bocheru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. All these were the sons of Azel.
The sons of his brother Eshek: Ulam was his firstborn, Jeush second, and Eliphelet third.
The sons of Ulam were {{gibbor|mighty men of valor}}, archers, and they had many sons and grandsons, 150 in all. All these were the descendants of Benjamin.

yachas.gloss: To enroll or reckon by genealogy; a genealogical registration or pedigree. The Chronicler uses this word repeatedly across chapters 1 to 9 to describe how families were recorded, underlining that Israel's identity after the exile rested on knowing whose family, and whose promise, they belonged to.

baal.gloss: Lord, master, owner, husband; also the proper name of the Canaanite storm god. Saul's household used this word in names like Esh-baal, man of the lord, and Merib-baal, the lord contends. Later Scripture, in 2 Samuel, replaces it with bosheth, shame, showing how thoroughly Baal worship had disgraced the word by the time of the monarchy's fall.

gibbor.gloss: Mighty, a mighty man, a warrior of valor. The word marks out Benjamin's fighting reputation here, in Ulam's skilled archers, and will reappear for David's own mighty men in chapter 11, quietly measuring every human gibbor against the one true and unfailing King.

basic.context: This chapter finishes the long run of genealogies that open 1 Chronicles by circling back to Benjamin, the tribe already introduced briefly in chapter 7, and tracing it in far greater detail, all the way down to Saul, Israel's first king. It lists Benjamin's founding sons, several branching family lines, some who were exiled even before the nation's later exile, others born in Moab, families who settled Jerusalem and Gibeon, and finally Saul's own household, ending in the skilled archers descended from Ulam. The chapter answers a quiet but important question for the Chronicler's first readers: who were we before the exile, and who are we now that some of us have returned?
basic.hermeneutics: This is genealogy, not narrative, so its message comes through selection, sequence, and placement rather than plot. The Chronicler is not merely filling in names; he is deliberately setting Saul's family at the very end of Benjamin's list, right before he turns, in chapters 9 and 10, to the account of Saul's death. Reading it well means asking why this list is here at all, and the answer is that it prepares the ground for the tragedy to come while also anchoring Benjamin's ongoing identity among God's people.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is stitched together by the language of {{yachas|genealogy}}, of families reckoned and recorded so a scattered people could know who they were. Saul's own family carries the word {{baal|baal}}, lord or master, in names like Esh-baal and Merib-baal, a word that later Scripture will not repeat once it becomes tied to Canaanite idolatry. And Benjamin's fighting reputation surfaces in the {{gibbor|mighty men}} of Ulam's line, a word that will return, transformed, around David.
basic.typology: Saul's genealogy, traced out in careful, almost affectionate detail, only to collapse in the next two chapters, stands as a shadow-picture of every merely human kingship: full of promise on paper, yet unable to endure. It throws into relief the genealogy of Judah already given in chapters 2 to 4, the line that runs to David and, at last, to Christ, the King whose house does not end in tragedy.
basic.christ: This chapter does not mention Christ directly, but it does its quiet work of contrast. Judah's genealogy has already been given at length; now Benjamin's genealogy is given at length too, ending in the king who will fail. The reader is meant to feel the difference: one line ends in disaster, the other, already marked out, will end in the everlasting throne of David's greater Son, Jesus, the King who never fails and whose kingdom cannot be shaken.
basic.god: God is the quiet keeper of every family here, none of them famous, most of them never mentioned again, yet each one recorded. He does not erase Benjamin for producing a failed king; He keeps this tribe within the story, so that generations later its descendants would stand alongside Judah among the remnant that returned. God's faithfulness to keep His people running through failure to fulfillment shows itself even in a list of names.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 9 introduces Saul himself as a young man of great promise. 2 Samuel 2:8 and 4:4 give the alternate names Ish-bosheth and Mephibosheth, showing the later discomfort with the word baal. Ezra 1:5 and Nehemiah 11:4-9 record that it was Judah and Benjamin together who resettled Jerusalem after the exile, the direct fulfillment of what this chapter's placement anticipates.
basic.oneStory: Benjamin's family tree, patiently traced to its tragic end in Saul, sets up the drama of the chapters that follow, while also rooting the Chronicler's own post-exilic readers, drawn from Judah and Benjamin alike, in a story bigger than one king's failure. The line that truly endures is not Saul's but David's, already recorded earlier, and it runs at last to Jesus Christ, the King whose house will never fall.

context.literary: This chapter closes the genealogical section of 1 Chronicles 1 to 9 by returning to Benjamin in far greater detail than chapter 7 gave it, deliberately building toward Saul's household as its final and climactic entry. The repetition of Benjamin's genealogy, once briefly in chapter 7 and now at length here, signals its importance: this is the tribe whose most famous son will occupy the very next chapters as a tragic figure.
context.historical: Benjamin held a peculiar place in Israel's story, a small tribe that nonetheless produced the nation's first king, and later, alongside Judah, formed the core of the Southern Kingdom and then of the community that returned from Babylonian exile. Naming towns like Jerusalem, Gibeon, Aijalon, and Lod situates these families geographically in territory Benjamin actually occupied, land the Chronicler's audience would have recognized as their own inheritance being resettled.
context.canonical: This genealogy functions as the hinge between the Chronicler's genealogical prologue and his narrative history proper. By ending with Saul, it directly prepares for 1 Chronicles 9:35-10:14, where Saul's genealogy is repeated once more immediately before his death is narrated, framing his story with his family so that his failure is felt as a family tragedy, not merely a private one.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record Benjamin's family lines in full, ending deliberately with Saul's household, so as to prepare the reader for the account of Saul's downfall and to affirm Benjamin's continuing, valued place among the people of God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter is descriptive record, not moral instruction; it prescribes nothing about how to live. What it does teach, by its placement and content, is that God keeps track of every family, that a tribe's identity is not destroyed by its king's failure, and that the reader should feel the coming contrast between Saul's collapsing house and David's enduring one.

debates.question: Why does Chronicles preserve the baal element in names like Esh-baal and Merib-baal when 2 Samuel replaces it with bosheth, shame?

views.label: Chronicles retains the older, unedited historical form
views.case: Baal originally simply meant lord or master and, especially in Israel's earliest monarchy, could even be used of the LORD Himself, Hosea 2:16; the Chronicler, writing generations after the exile had already settled the question of Baal worship for good, had no need to euphemize the ancient family names and simply preserved the genealogical record as he received it.

views.label: Samuel's bosheth substitution reflects the shame later attached to the name
views.case: By the time these names reached their final written form in Samuel, or in the scribal tradition behind it, the word baal had become so identified with Canaanite idolatry that pious scribes substituted bosheth wherever it appeared in personal names, both to avoid honoring a false god in speech and to mark the disgrace idolatry had brought on Saul's house.

debates.bearing: Either way, the underlying reality does not change: Saul's household bore names built on a word that could mean lord generically or the Canaanite god specifically, and the son who carried one of them, Merib-baal, later received remarkable, unearned kindness from David, 2 Samuel 9, a small foretaste of the mercy that reaches into every compromised and disgraced house through Christ.

debates.question: Why record Benjamin's genealogy in such detail right before narrating Saul's downfall?

views.label: To prepare a stark contrast with David's already-established line
views.case: Chapters 2 to 4 have already traced Judah's genealogy in detail, culminating in David; placing Benjamin's genealogy, ending in Saul, immediately before the account of his death in chapters 9 and 10 invites the reader to feel the difference between the king who failed and the king God had already chosen, whose line the Chronicler treasures far more.

views.label: To honor Benjamin's ongoing place in the covenant community
views.case: The Chronicler is not interested only in condemning Saul; he takes real care to record Benjamin's families in full, showing that this tribe, despite producing a failed king, remains a full and valued part of Israel, a tribe that, alongside Judah, will physically resettle Jerusalem, 1 Chronicles 9:3.

debates.bearing: Both readings work together: the genealogy soberly prepares the reader for Saul's tragic end while also affirming that a tribe's, or a family's, worst chapter does not define its entire future in God's purposes.

study.originalLanguages: The whole chapter is an exercise in {{yachas|genealogy}}, the careful reckoning of family lines that let a scattered, exiled people know exactly who they were and where they belonged. Inside Saul's own family, the word {{baal|baal}}, lord, surfaces in Esh-baal and Merib-baal, a title later Scripture will not use of Saul's line again once it becomes irreversibly tied to Canaanite idolatry, replaced instead with bosheth, shame. And at the very end, the {{gibbor|mighty men}} of Ulam's line, skilled archers within Benjamin, anticipate the greater company of mighty men who will soon gather, not around Saul, but around David.

typology.type: Saul's genealogy, carefully traced yet ending in collapse
typology.fulfillment: A shadow of every merely human kingship, thrown into relief by the enduring line of David, fulfilled in the everlasting kingdom of Christ, Luke 1:32-33.

typology.type: Benjamin's continued place among the resettled remnant
typology.fulfillment: God's refusal to discard a whole people for the sin of their king, a pattern completed in Christ's welcome of all who come to Him, Judah and Benjamin, Jew and Gentile alike, Ephesians 2:14-16.

study.christ: This genealogical chapter does not name Christ, yet it does its quiet, structural work: Judah's messianic line has already been laid out in chapters 2 to 4, and now Benjamin's line is laid out too, ending deliberately in the king who will shortly be shown dying in disgrace. The contrast is the point. Where Saul's house collapses under judgment, David's house, already recorded, will stand forever, fulfilled at last in Jesus, the Son of David whose throne cannot fail, Luke 1:32-33. Even Merib-baal, grandson of the failed king, becomes a small preview of gospel grace, welcomed permanently at David's own table, 2 Samuel 9:7, 13, a picture of how the true King draws even the house of His rival into His mercy.
study.god: God is shown here as the quiet keeper of ordinary families, none of them famous, many never named again, yet every one recorded before Him. He does not erase Benjamin for producing a failed king, nor does He erase Saul's own descendants from the record, even Merib-baal, whose name still carries the shame of baal. God's faithfulness works itself out not only in dramatic acts of judgment and deliverance but in the patient, unglamorous preservation of family lines, so that centuries later a remnant of Benjamin, alongside Judah, could return to Jerusalem and know they still belonged to His story.

crossReferences.note: Introduces Saul himself, tall and full of promise, the young man whose family this chapter now traces to its tragic end.
Give the alternate names Ish-bosheth and Mephibosheth, showing the later substitution of bosheth, shame, for baal in Saul's household.
David shows covenant kindness to Merib-baal, Mephibosheth, grandson of Saul, a picture of grace reaching into a disgraced house.
Record that it was Judah and Benjamin together who resettled Jerusalem after the exile, the fulfillment this genealogy anticipates.

study.oneStory: Benjamin's family tree, patiently traced across seven family groups and ending in Saul's own household, quietly prepares the reader for the tragedy the Chronicler will tell in chapters 9 and 10. But it also does something hopeful: it roots the Chronicler's own post-exilic audience, drawn from Judah and Benjamin together, in a story that does not end with a failed king. The line that truly endures is David's, already recorded earlier in this same book, and it runs at last to Jesus Christ, the King whose house, unlike Saul's, will never fall.

quiz.q: Why does the Chronicler pause to give Benjamin's genealogy again here, at greater length than in chapter 7?

options.text: To trace the line down to Saul's own household, setting up the tragic account of his death in chapters 9 and 10.
options.note: The chapter's placement, right before Saul's death is narrated, is deliberate literary preparation.

options.text: Because Benjamin was a large and important tribe worth recording in full.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it misses the specific link this list forges to Saul's coming downfall.

options.text: Because the Chronicler had simply run out of new material to record.
options.note: A dismissive half-truth; the Chronicler is working with clear purpose, not filler.

options.text: Because Benjamin was about to be permanently excluded from Israel.
options.note: False; Benjamin remains part of Israel and later resettles Jerusalem alongside Judah.

quiz.q: What do names like Esh-baal and Merib-baal preserve, and how do they differ from their forms in 2 Samuel?

options.text: The original names built on baal, lord or master; 2 Samuel later substitutes bosheth, shame, reflecting how Baal worship had disgraced that title.
options.note: Verses 33-34 compared with 2 Samuel 2:8 and 4:4 show exactly this substitution.

options.text: Ordinary family names that simply reflected that generation's culture.
options.note: Partly true, but it misses the theological weight of the later name change.

options.text: Names Saul deliberately chose to honor the Canaanite god Baal.
options.note: Overstates the case; baal could simply mean lord, and deliberate idol-worship in the naming is not stated in the text.

options.text: Names invented by the Chronicler with no historical basis.
options.note: False; the names are part of the received genealogical tradition, confirmed across both Samuel and Chronicles.

quiz.q: Where does Benjamin fit into the story of the returned exiles, the Chronicler's first audience?

options.text: Benjamin, alongside Judah, was one of the two tribes that actually resettled Jerusalem after the exile, so this genealogy roots the Chronicler's own audience in the story.
options.note: Ezra 2 and Nehemiah 11 confirm Judah and Benjamin as the returning core of the community.

options.text: Benjamin remained part of Israel's story even after Saul's failure.
options.note: True, but vague; it misses the specific historical detail of the return from exile.

options.text: Benjamin merged entirely into Judah and lost its own tribal identity.
options.note: A half-truth; Benjamin was closely allied with Judah but is still named separately in the records of the return.

options.text: Benjamin was cut off from Israel entirely because of Saul's sin.
options.note: False; the text and later history show Benjamin continuing as a recognized tribe.

quiz.q: What is the literary effect of ending this chapter with Saul's genealogy right before chapters 9 and 10?

options.text: It sets the stage for the fall of Saul, contrasting his tragic line with the enduring line of David already traced in earlier chapters.
options.note: The Chronicler builds a deliberate contrast between Judah's line, chapters 2-4, and Benjamin's, ending here in tragedy.

options.text: It simply shows that Saul had many descendants.
options.note: True as a bare fact, but it misses the contrastive, preparatory purpose of the placement.

options.text: It proves that Saul's family continued to reign as kings after him.
options.note: Misleading; Saul's dynasty as king ended with him, even though his family line continued.

options.text: It has no connection to the chapters that follow.
options.note: False; the very next chapters narrate Saul's genealogy again and then his death.

quiz.q: How does this genealogy point forward to Christ?

options.text: By setting up the contrast between Saul's failed kingship and the everlasting line of David, which finds its fulfillment in Jesus, the true and unfailing King.
options.note: The chapter's placement makes the reader feel the difference between a house that fails and a house that endures forever in Christ.

options.text: By showing God's care in remembering every family, a pattern of grace fulfilled in Christ's care for His people.
options.note: True and fitting, but more generic; it misses the specific Saul-versus-David contrast the chapter is building.

options.text: By showing that a king from any tribe would have served Israel equally well.
options.note: Misplaces the emphasis; the text is building toward one particular chosen line, Judah's, not treating all tribes as equal candidates for kingship.

options.text: By showing that genealogies have no bearing on the gospel story.
options.note: False; the genealogies of Chronicles are foundational to tracing the promised line that leads to Christ.

questions: Benjamin's genealogy quietly sets up Saul's tragic fall two chapters later. Where in your own life have you seen God preparing you for a hard lesson before you even knew it was coming?

Even the tribe of the failed king, Benjamin, found its place again among the resettled remnant after the exile. How does that shape the way you think about your own past failures and God's willingness to keep you in His story?

The names Esh-baal and Merib-baal remind us how easily God's people can absorb the language and values of the cultures around them. Where might you be unconsciously borrowing the world's names for what only God should be called?
This long list of ordinary, largely forgotten names still mattered enough for God to record. What does that tell you about how God sees the details of your own life and family?
Saul's line ends in tragedy, but David's line, already traced earlier in Chronicles, endures forever in Christ. Whose house are you building your hope on today?

translationNotes.note: BSB footnotes explain that Esh-baal is also called Ish-bosheth in 2 Samuel 2:8, and Merib-baal is called Mephibosheth in 2 Samuel 4:4; the change from baal, lord, to bosheth, shame, reflects later discomfort with the word baal.
Some Septuagint manuscripts read the father of Gibeon where the Hebrew reads simply Gibeon, understood as the founder of the town; compare 1 Chronicles 9:35.

The sons of Ulam are noted as skilled archers among mighty men of valor, a martial reputation within Benjamin that echoes the tribe's skilled left-handed warriors elsewhere in Scripture, such as Judges 20:16.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 8:1-40. One em dash in the source at verse 40 was converted to a comma; no dashes remain in this file.
